macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Beit Shammai and the Institution of Mi'un (Refusal)

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and introduction to Perek Beit Shammai
Definition and mechanics of mi'un: retroactive dissolution of marriage
Stages of marriage and when mi'un can be exercised

macro_segments.title: Beit Shammai vs. Beit Hillel on Mi'un: Arusot vs. Nesuot

micro_segments.title: Beit Shammai's position: mi'un only for arusot, not nesuot
Beit Hillel's position: mi'un permitted for both arusot and nesuot
Beit Shammai's concern: daughters of Israel should not be treated as hefker
Student discussion: understanding the injunction and its practical effects

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis of Beit Shammai's Reasoning: Ein Tenai BeNisuin

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's explanation: stipulations cannot work at the stage of nisuin
Distinction between tnai (stipulation) and ta'ut (fundamental error)
Concern that allowing mi'un for nesuot will mislead people into thinking tenai works
Tosafot's point: despite stipulations, ein tenai benisuin is not absolute

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Exploration: When Exactly Does Mi'un Cease to Apply?

micro_segments.title: Case of nichnesah lechuppah velo niv'ala: after chuppah but before sex
Why no tenai works at chuppah stage either: lo nicha lei d'teihavei chuppat issura
Case of masrah lishluchei ha-av: before chuppah but transferred to husband's agents
Gemara's response: lo plug rabanan—not distinguishing between these stages

macro_segments.title: Alternative Explanation by Rabba and Rav Yosef: Ein Adam Oseh Bei'ato Bei'at Zenut

micro_segments.title: Concern about husband's willingness to consummate marriage if mi'un remains possible
Marital sex must not be subject to retroactive nullification through mi'un
Beit Hillel's rebuttal: keivan d'ika kiddushin v'ketubah, context of institution prevents zenut status
Implications for kehunah and woman's status if retroactively never married

macro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Financial Explanation: Peiri (Usufruct Rights)

micro_segments.title: Beit Shammai's concern: husband will exploit nichsei milug if mi'un threat exists
Beit Hillel's counterargument: threat of mi'un encourages husband to invest in property
Rava's additional concern: wedding expenses and social standing implications
Tosafot's point: both parties desire the wedding celebration regardless of outcome

macro_segments.title: Mi'un and Yibum: Can She Refuse a Brother-in-Law After Husband's Death?

micro_segments.title: Beit Shammai's position: mi'un only works against ba'al, not yabam
Rabbi Oshaya's interpretation: mi'un works for ma'amar but not for zikah
Rav Chisda's explanation: distinction between willingly entered zikah (ma'amar) and inherited zikah
Gemara's challenge: why not mi'un for bi'as ketanah if it works for ma'amar?

macro_segments.title: Ulla's Alternative View: Mi'un Works Against Zikah and Brother-in-Law

micro_segments.title: Ulla's position: mena'enet af le-zikato—mi'un severs original marriage even retroactively
Rava's challenge: case of tzarat ervah (co-wife) and prohibitions
Rami bar Yechezkel's source: mi'un to ba'al permits marriage to his father; mi'un to yabam does not

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Status at Moment of Husband's Death Becomes Instantiated

micro_segments.title: Principle: bi-sh'at nefilah nirit kechallato—status at death becomes real regardless of later mi'un
Application: tzarat ervah status cannot be completely undone even if mi'un is performed later
Explanation: retroactive dissolution does not undo the status created at moment of death

macro_segments.title: Rav vs. Shmuel: Can Mi'un Against One Brother Free Her From Others?

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: mi'un b'zeh asurah l'zeh—refusal of one disqualifies from all
Rationale: analogous to gevias get—one brother's action affects all
Shmuel's position: mi'un b'zeh muteres l'zeh—she can still do yibum with other brother
Distinction: she (not he) is acting; her specific rejection of one brother does not affect others
Rav Ashi's view: mi'un meaningless even to that one brother if multiple brothers exist

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Final Analysis: Collective Zikah and the All-or-Nothing Principle

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's tradition: mi'un b'zeh muteres l'achim—permitted to brothers after refusal of one
Abaye's identification: Rav (not Shmuel) disagrees with individual refusal doctrine
Rava's identification: Rabbi Oshaya disagrees because he rejects mi'un for zikah entirely
Resolution: ein mi'un l'chatzi zikah—cannot partially sever collective zikah inherited from deceased husband
Conclusion: must reject all brothers or none; zikah is inherited as single unit from original husband

topical_tags.term: Mi'un (refusal by minor wife)
Arusah vs. Nesuah (betrothed vs. married stages)
Ein tenai benisuin (no stipulations at marriage stage)
Ein adam oseh bei'ato bei'at zenut (marital sex not to be redefined as fornication)
Nichsei milug (wife's property with husband's usufruct rights)
Yibum and Chalitzah (levirate marriage and release)
Zikah (widow's bond to deceased husband's brothers)
Ma'amar (formal declaration of yibum by brother-in-law)
Tzarat ervah (sister-in-law as sexual prohibitions relative)
Kiddushei ketanah (marriage of minor without father)
Lo yeheyu benot Yisrael hefker (daughters of Israel must not be treated as ownerless)
Chuppah (wedding canopy and cohabitation marker)
Beit Shammai vs. Beit Hillel debates
Rav Yehuda Amar Shmuel (source attribution pattern)
Rav vs. Shmuel (rabbinic dispute on mi'un and multiple brothers)
Rabbi Oshaya's position on ma'amar vs. zikah
Rav Chisda's distinction between willingly entered and inherited zikah
Ulla's alternative: mi'un severs zikah entirely
Rami bar Yechezkel's source on mi'un differences
Rav Pappa's financial rationale
Rava's explanation of wedding expense concerns
Bi-sh'at nefilah nirit (status instantiated at moment of death)
Gevias get (collection of get by one brother affects others)
Rabbi Yochanan's tradition on mi'un to brothers
Ein mi'un l'chatzi zikah (cannot partially sever zikah)
